comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in SegFormer-B4 performance claim
comments.quote: For example, SegFormer-B4 achieves 50.3% mIoU on ADE20K with 64M parameters, being 5 × smaller and 2.2% better than the previous best method.
comments.explanation: In the Abstract, the authors state that SegFormer-B4 is '2.2% better than the previous best method'. However, Figure 1 and the surrounding context (and standard benchmarking in the paper) indicate that the previous best method (typically cited as SETR or similar) achieves significantly lower mIoU (e.g., ~48.6% as per the table in Figure 1). A 2.2% absolute improvement over 48.6% is ~50.8%, not 50.3%. Furthermore, 50.3% relative to the range of improvements typically discussed suggests an ambiguity between absolute percentage point gain and relative improvement. Given that the Abstract also mentions the model eventually hits 51.8% mIoU, the specific claim '2.2% better' at the '50.3% mIoU' mark appears to be a numerical mismatch or a calculation error.

comments.title: Underspecified mathematical notation in decoder formulation
comments.quote: predict the segmentation mask with a [H] 4 [×][W] 4 [×][ N][cls]_[resolution, where] _[ N][cls]_[is the number of categories.]
comments.explanation: The OCR-extracted text here is garbled, and while the context suggests the output is H/4 x W/4 x N_cls, the notation '[H] 4 [×][W] 4' is mathematically ambiguous. It is standard in semantic segmentation to denote reduced resolution as H/4 × W/4 (where the denominator is the stride). While the intent is clear from the context of segmentation architectures, the formal specification of the output shape in the methodology section is presented inconsistently or with corrupted notation that renders the formulaic definition unreadable.

comments.title: Inconsistency in ADE20K state-of-the-art claim
comments.quote: SegFormer sets new a state-of-the-art in terms of efficiency, accuracy and robustness... As shown in Figure 1, SegFormer sets new a state-of-the-art... SegFormer achieves a new state-of-the-art 51.0% mIoU
comments.explanation: The paper makes several claims regarding the 'new state-of-the-art' on ADE20K. In the Introduction, it mentions 51.8% mIoU, the caption of Figure 1 says 51.0% mIoU, and the main text says 50.3% for B4. While these represent different models (B4 vs B5), the repetition of 'new state-of-the-art' alongside three different conflicting values for the same dataset creates potential confusion for the reader regarding the actual performance peak of the architecture at the time of writing.

comments.title: Missing Formula in Section 3.2
comments.quote: This lets us formulate the decoder as: [line break where equation is expected]
comments.explanation: The text explicitly states 'This lets us formulate the decoder as:' followed by a sentence ending with the definition of M and the Linear function, but the actual mathematical expression (the equation for the decoder) is completely missing from the provided text. This is a crucial procedural definition for the 'All-MLP decoder' mentioned in the title of the section, making the architectural contribution underspecified.

comments.title: Unspecified Mix-FFN Formula
comments.quote: Mix-FFN is formulated as: [line break where equation is expected]
comments.explanation: Similar to the decoder section, the paper presents a colon indicating that a formal definition for 'Mix-FFN' follows, but the equation itself is omitted from the text provided. Because Mix-FFN is a central component of the positional-encoding-free design, its absence makes the implementation of the core architectural contribution ambiguous.

comments.title: Missing Self-Attention Equation
comments.quote: the self-attention is estimated as: [line break where equation is expected]
comments.explanation: The text signals the definition of the self-attention formula, but the LaTeX expression for Attention(Q, K, V) is missing. While the formula is standard in the field, it is expected in a technical paper, and its omission disrupts the flow of the mathematical justification provided in Section 3.1.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of Sequence Reduction
comments.quote: We instead adopt the sequence reduction process introduced in [8]. This process uses a reduction ratio R to reduce the length of the sequence of as follows:
comments.explanation: The sentence ends with 'as follows:' but contains no formula. Furthermore, the prose describes the process generally, but without the missing equation, a reader cannot discern the specific tensor operations (the sequence of Reshape, Linear, and normalization) implemented. Given that complexity reduction is a core claim of the paper, this omission is significant.

comments.title: OCR Distortions in Equation Section
comments.quote: where $K$ is the sequence to be reduced, Reshape($N/R, C*R$)(K) refers to reshape $K$ to the one with shape of $N/R \times (C*R)$, and Linear($C_{in}, C_{out}$)(·) refers to a linear layer
comments.explanation: The OCR extraction has introduced significant distortion to the mathematical notation. Specifically, 'Reshape(N/R, C*R)(K)' contains non-standard delimiters and formatting that obscure the actual tensor operation dimension. While the concept of sequence reduction is standard in papers like PVT, the provided text here is garbled to the point of being mathematically incoherent in the printed version. It hinders the replicability of the specific reduction mechanism described.

comments.title: Logical Inconsistency regarding 'no positional encoding'
comments.quote: our encoder can easily adapt to arbitrary test resolutions without impacting the performance. In addition, the hierarchical part enables the encoder to generate both high-resolution fine features and low-resolution coarse features, this is in contrast to ViT that can only produce single low-resolution feature maps with fixed resolutions.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that their model is 'positional-encoding-free.' However, they rely on 3x3 depth-wise convolutions within the Mix-FFN to introduce 'positional information.' While this is a clever design choice intended to avoid the fixed-size grid limitations of standard ViT, it is technically acting as a form of relative positional bias (a common technique in shifted-window or convolution-augmented transformers). While the authors acknowledge this design choice, claiming it is 'positional-encoding-free' is somewhat misleading as it merely moves the positional inductive bias from a global PE to an implicit relative bias via convolution. This could mislead a reader about the fundamental nature of the architecture.

comments.title: Numerical Mismatch in Table 1a/1b
comments.quote: Given these results, we choose C = 256 for our real-time models SegFormer-B0, B1 and C = 768 for the rest.
comments.explanation: The text states they chose C=256 for B0 and B1, and C=768 for the rest. However, looking at the provided Table 1b, the row labels for C include 256, 512, 768, 1024, 2048. If the authors claim C=768 is the standard for the 'rest', it is ambiguous whether 'rest' refers to B2-B5, as the table rows appear to be exploring the hyperparameter space rather than listing model models B0-B5 explicitly. Furthermore, the params in Table 1a for MiT-B4 and B5 show a constant 3.3M/3.3M decoder parameter count, which contradicts the logic that different encoder backbones (with different output channel dimensions) would require different decoder MLP capacities to maintain efficiency.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in Attention formula
comments.quote: Attention(Q, K, V) = Softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k))V
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, the paper references the standard attention formula but fails to explicitly define the scalar normalization factor $d_k$ relative to the previously introduced channel dimensions $C_i$. While industry standards often assume $d_k$ is the dimension of the key/query, the paper later defines $C$ as the channel dimension of the MLP decoder and $C_i$ as the encoder channel dimension. Given the earlier mention of $N=H \times W$, the lack of explicit connection between the attention dimension (often $C_i / \text{heads}$) and the defined parameter $C_i$ could lead to confusion for a reader trying to reconcile the dimension of the head projections with the hierarchical channel notation.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Sequence Reduction notation
comments.quote: Reshape(N/R, C*R)(K)
comments.explanation: The notation for sequence reduction shows $K$ being reshaped into $(N/R, C*R)$, which implies the channel dimension is multiplied by $R$. However, the subsequent description of the output channel dimension mentions a 'Linear layer... generating $C_{out}$-dimensional tensor'. The notation $C*R$ is ambiguous: does it mean the channel dimension $C$ is scaled by $R$ before the linear projection? The text later states the resulting $K$ has dimensions $N/R \times C$, but the intermediate step $C*R$ is structurally opaque regarding whether $C$ is the original channel size or a projected size. While the logic is likely 'reshape then project', the mathematical notation appears to conflate these two operations.

comments.title: Logical gap in ERF analysis for MLP decoder
comments.quote: The same decoder design, however, does not work well on CNN backbones since the overall receptive field is upper bounded by the limited one at Stage-4, and we will verify this later in Table 1d,
comments.explanation: The authors claim their All-MLP decoder is ineffective on CNN backbones because of the 'ERF upper bound'. They reference 'Table 1d' to support this, but the provided context does not include a 'Table 1d'. While the theoretical argument (CNNs have restricted receptive fields compared to global/local attention combinations) is standard, the claim would be much stronger if the empirical evidence promised via the missing table was present. Without the table, the assertion that the decoder *cannot* work well with CNNs—as opposed to just performing worse—remains an unsupported, potentially overstated claim.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in Mix-FFN formula
comments.quote: x_out = MLP(GELU(Conv3x3(x_in))) + x_in
comments.explanation: In the summary and earlier text, the authors define Mix-FFN as including a 3x3 depth-wise convolution. However, the provided equation in the text uses 'Conv3x3', which in standard deep learning literature (and without explicit qualifications like 'DWConv') implies a standard convolution. While the prose clarifies that they use depth-wise convolutions for efficiency, the discrepancy between the prose and the formal definition of the equation could mislead a reader attempting to replicate the model architecture.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing of latency improvement
comments.quote: On Citysapces, our lightweight model, SegFormer-B0, without accelerated implementations such as TensorRT, yields 71.9% mIoU at 48 FPS, which, compared to ICNet [11], represents a relative improvement of 60% and 4.2% in latency and performance, respectively.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'relative improvement of 60% ... in latency' is ambiguous since latency measures execution time; an 'improvement' typically implies reduction, but the authors may be conflating latency with throughput (FPS), which would represent a speed increase. Without the baseline FPS/latency for ICNet, the exact speedup factor cannot be independently verified. While the intent to highlight efficiency is clear, this ambiguity could mislead readers benchmarking speedup claims against other methods. Severity is moderate given the central role of efficiency metrics in the paper.

comments.title: Inconsistent and non-standard reporting of mIoU improvements
comments.quote: Our largest model, SegFormer-B5, yields 84.0% mIoU, which represents a relative 1.8% mIoU improvement while being 5 × faster than SETR [7]." / "being 5 × smaller and 2.2% better than the previous best method." vs "establishes a new state-of-the-art of 51.8%, which is 1.6% mIoU better than SETR
comments.explanation: The paper inconsistently reports performance gains across sections. The phrasing 'relative 1.8% mIoU improvement' is non-standard; gains are conventionally reported as absolute percentage points (+1.8 mIoU) or as a clear relative increase over a specified baseline. Additionally, improvement margins on ADE20K vary between the abstract (2.2%) and Section 4.3 (1.6%), which conflict with the actual gaps derived from Table 1 (~1.7% and ~3.2% respectively). This terminology slip and numerical inconsistency undermine precise benchmarking of accuracy-efficiency claims.

comments.title: Numerical discrepancy between figure caption and main text
comments.quote: Figure 1: Performance vs. model efficiency on ADE20K. SegFormer achieves a new state-of-the-art 51.0% mIoU while being significantly more efficient than previous methods.\n\nOn ADE20K, this model sets a new state-of-the-art of 51.8% mIoU while being 4 × smaller than SETR.
comments.explanation: The figure caption reports a state-of-the-art mIoU of 51.0% for SegFormer-B5, whereas the introduction text claims 51.8% for the same model on the same dataset. While this likely stems from differing validation/test splits, cropping strategies, or evaluation protocols, the inconsistency in introductory material creates confusion regarding the exact reported performance. Reconciling this requires consulting the full experimental setup.

comments.title: Missing sequence reduction equation
comments.quote: This process uses a reduction ratio R to reduce the length of the sequence of as follows:\n\nwhere K is the sequence to be reduced, Reshape( [N] R[, C][ · R])([ K])(] refers to reshape[ K][to the one with] shape of [N] R[×] ([ C][· R])[)][,
comments.explanation: Following 'as follows:', the actual reduction operation is missing. The surrounding text is heavily OCR-corrupted and verbally describes Reshape and Linear layers but contains duplication errors and fails to show the tensor permutation/projection pipeline. Without the explicit update rule showing how sequence K is transformed and how the linear layer operates on the reshaped tensor, the claimed $O((N/R)^2)$ complexity gain cannot be mathematically verified or reproduced.

comments.title: Missing Mix-FFN architectural formulation
comments.quote: Mix-FFN is formulated as:\n\nwhere **x** _in_ is the feature from the self-attention module.
comments.explanation: The section introduces the positional-encoding-free design by mixing a 3x3 convolution with the standard MLP/FFN, but the defining equation is absent. The subsequent text describes the components and motivation but omits the exact computational graph (e.g., residual connections, additive/multiplicative mixing pattern, depth-wise vs. pointwise conv structure). This lack of formal specification leaves a core architectural contribution underspecified for exact reproduction.

comments.title: Mismatch between stated dataset count and listed datasets
comments.quote: We used four public datasets: Cityscapes [69], ADE20K [70], and COCO-Stuff [71].
comments.explanation: The authors explicitly state they used 'four public datasets,' but only three are subsequently listed and described. No fourth dataset is introduced or evaluated in the surrounding text or experimental sections. This appears to be a straightforward typographical error that creates a factual inconsistency in the setup description.

comments.title: Non-standard terminology for polynomial learning rate schedule
comments.quote: The learning rate was set to an initial value of 0.00006 and then used a “poly” LR schedule with factor 1.0 by default.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'poly LR schedule with factor 1.0' is technically incorrect. Polynomial learning rate schedules are parameterized by a `power` exponent ($lr = lr_{init} \times (1 - iter/T)^{power}$), whereas 'factor' typically refers to exponential decay or ReduceLROnPlateau schedulers. While a power of 1.0 yields a linear decay (likely the intent), using 'factor' conflicts with standard deep learning framework terminology and could mislead implementers.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding 'negligible computational overhead'
comments.quote: Our MLP decoder is more compact and less computationally demanding than the one in SETR. This leads to a _negligible_ computational overhead. In contrast, SETR requires heavy decoders with multiple 3 _×_ 3 convolutions.
comments.explanation: The claim that the SegFormer decoder 'leads to a negligible computational overhead' is semantically inverted relative to the preceding comparative clause. Since the decoder is explicitly described as less demanding than SETR's, it should reduce overall cost rather than create 'overhead.' While the intended meaning (the decoder adds minimal parameters/computation to the pipeline) is clear from context, the phrasing is logically confusing and could momentarily mislead readers.

comments.title: Underspecified patch merging parameters across encoder stages
comments.quote: In our experiments, we set _K_ = 7, _S_ = 4, _P_ = 3 ,and _K_ = 3, _S_ = 2, _P_ = 1 to perform overlapping patch merging to produces features with the same size as the non-overlapping process.
comments.explanation: Two distinct sets of overlapping patch merging parameters are listed, but the text fails to explicitly map each set to specific encoder stages. While standard hierarchical Vision Transformer practices suggest mapping the larger kernel/stride to earlier stages and the smaller set to later stages, the authors do not specify the stage-wise configuration. This leaves the resolution hierarchy underspecified, requiring inference or external code to precisely reproduce.

comments.title: Potentially misaligned parameter counts in Table 1b
comments.quote: Table 1b shows performance, flops, and parameters as a function of this dimension. We can observe that setting _C_ = 256 provides a very competitive performance and computational cost. The performance increases as _C_ increases; however, it leads to larger and less efficient models.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that increasing the MLP decoder channel dimension C linearly scales parameters and FLOPs. However, the reported parameter counts in Table 1b are non-monotonic: C=512 reports 39.8M, C=768 drops to 27.5M, and C=1024 rises to 29.6M. This contradicts both the expected linear scaling and the authors' own description, indicating a likely table formatting error or reporting typo that could mislead readers evaluating the parameter-accuracy trade-off.

comments.title: Unverifiable relative improvement claim on Cityscapes-C corruptions
comments.quote: yielding a relative improvement of up to 588% on Gaussian Noise and up to 295% on snow weather.
comments.explanation: The text claims relative improvements of 588% (Gaussian Noise) and 295% (Snow) over previous methods. Cross-referencing with Table 5, these percentages do not match calculations against any listed baseline (e.g., vs. DLv3+ MBv2 yields ~803%/546%; vs. DLv3+ X71 yields ~5.3%/25%). Without specifying the exact baseline or calculation method, the claim is ambiguous and could mislead readers regarding the actual magnitude of robustness gains.

comments.title: Typos and grammatical inconsistencies in results and analysis
comments.quote: SegFormer-B5 archives the best IoU of 84.0%" / "with a significant stronger local attention" / "compared to DeeplabV3+ (MobileNetV2), SegFormer-B0 is 7.4 FPS, which is faster and keeps 3.4% better mIoU.
comments.explanation: Several typographical and grammatical errors detract from clarity. 'archives' is a typo for 'achieves', 'significant stronger' should be 'significantly stronger', and phrasing like 'SegFormer-B0 is 7.4 FPS' lacks a proper verb. While these do not impact technical validity, they reduce prose quality and could momentarily confuse readers parsing quantitative claims. These also resolve earlier mentions of dropped prepositions/verbs in OCR extractions.

comments.explanation: I initially paused at the phrase 'relative improvement of 60% ... in latency.' Since latency measures execution time, an 'improvement' typically implies a reduction. It is unclear whether the authors mean latency was reduced by 60% (i.e., 2.5× faster) or if they are conflating latency with throughput (FPS), which would instead represent a 60% increase in speed. Given standard conventions in computer vision reporting, efficiency claims precisely distinguish between percentage reduction in latency and percentage increase in throughput. Without ICNet's baseline FPS or latency, the exact speedup factor cannot be independently verified or compared. While the authors' intent is clearly to highlight efficiency gains, this ambiguity could mislead readers attempting to benchmark the speedup claim against other methods. This is a moderate issue because precise efficiency metrics are central to the paper's core contribution.

comments.title: Inconsistent terminology for performance improvement
comments.quote: Our largest model, SegFormer-B5, yields 84.0% mIoU, which represents a relative 1.8% mIoU improvement while being 5 × faster than SETR [7].
comments.explanation: The authors state a 'relative 1.8% mIoU improvement.' In semantic segmentation literature, gains are conventionally reported as absolute percentage points (e.g., +1.8 mIoU) or as a relative percentage increase over the baseline. Mixing 'relative' with a value expressed in percentage points creates ambiguity. Given the final score of 84.0%, the previous best was likely around 82.2%, which would be an absolute improvement of 1.8 percentage points. If it were strictly relative, the baseline would need to be specified, and the improvement would be calculated as a fraction of that baseline. This phrasing is non-standard and could confuse readers when accurately benchmarking against other works. I checked the context and figure tables, which consistently report absolute mIoU values, confirming this is likely a terminology slip rather than a methodological error. Severity is minor as it appears in an introductory summary and can be easily corrected.

comments.title: Numerical discrepancy between figure caption and main text
comments.quote:
Figure 1: Performance vs. model efficiency on ADE20K. SegFormer achieves a new state-of-the-art 51.0% mIoU while being significantly more efficient than previous methods.

On ADE20K, this model sets a new state-of-the-art of 51.8% mIoU while being 4 × smaller than SETR.
comments.explanation: The figure caption reports a state-of-the-art mIoU of 51.0% for SegFormer, whereas the introduction text claims 51.8% for the same model (SegFormer-B5) on the same dataset. In practice, this discrepancy commonly arises from differences between validation and test-dev splits, cropping strategies, or evaluation protocols. I reviewed the provided experimental context, which mentions varied crop sizes and evaluation settings but does not explicitly reconcile these two numbers in the passage. A careful reader might assume a typographical error or question which evaluation split corresponds to the claimed SOTA. While the difference is likely resolvable by consulting the full experimental setup, the inconsistency in the introductory material momentarily undermines clarity regarding the exact reported performance. I flag this as minor since it does not undermine the methodology or conclusions, but clarity in reporting key metrics is important for reproducibility.

comments.title: Missing baseline self-attention complexity formula
comments.quote: Recall that in the original multi-head self-attention, each of the heads _Q, K, V_ have the same dimensions _N × C_ , where _N_ = _H × W_ is the length of the sequence, the self-attention is estimated as:
comments.explanation: The text introduces the quadratic complexity bottleneck of standard self-attention but abruptly stops before providing the mathematical expression (typically $O(N^2 D)$ or similar). I initially wondered if the formula was lost to OCR noise, but after checking the surrounding claims about efficiency and the paper summary, it is clear the equation is simply absent. The authors correctly identify the $O(N^2)$ limitation, but skipping the baseline formula makes the subsequent complexity reduction claim slightly disjointed. While the missing expression is standard knowledge in the field, its omission breaks the formal derivation flow and leaves the efficiency claim mathematically anchored to nothing in the text.

Without the explicit update rule, a reader cannot verify the exact operation, which undermines the reproducibility of the claimed efficiency gain.

comments.title: Missing Mix-FFN architectural formulation
comments.quote:
Mix-FFN is formulated as:

where **x** _in_ is the feature from the self-attention module.
comments.explanation: The section introduces the positional-encoding-free design by mixing a 3x3 convolution with the standard MLP/FFN. However, the defining equation is absent. The subsequent text describes the components and their motivation but does not specify the exact computational graph (e.g., whether the conv is applied before/after the MLP, if it uses residual connections, or how depth-wise vs pointwise convs are structured). While the intuition is sound and aligns with the paper's claim of parameter efficiency, the lack of a formal formulation leaves the architectural details underspecified for exact reproduction. A careful reader would need to guess the residual/additive mixing pattern or search for it elsewhere, which is a notable gap for a core methodological contribution.

comments.title: Mismatch between stated dataset count and listed datasets
comments.quote: We used four public datasets: Cityscapes [69], ADE20K [70], and COCO-Stuff [71].
comments.explanation: I initially noticed the authors explicitly state they used 'four public datasets,' but only three are subsequently listed and described in the paragraph. I checked the surrounding text and the paper's experimental sections to see if a fourth dataset (e.g., ADE20K-test or a domain-specific split) was mentioned elsewhere in the passage. No fourth dataset is introduced or evaluated in this section. This appears to be a straightforward typographical error where 'three' was intended. It does not affect the methodology or reported results, but it is a factual inconsistency in the setup description.

comments.title: Non-standard terminology for polynomial learning rate schedule
comments.quote: The learning rate was set to an initial value of 0.00006 and then used a “poly” LR schedule with factor 1.0 by default.
comments.explanation: I was confused by the phrase 'poly LR schedule with factor 1.0.' In standard deep learning frameworks (e.g., PyTorch, MMSegmentation, which the authors explicitly cite using), a polynomial (`poly`) learning rate schedule is parameterized by a `power` exponent: $lr = lr_{init} \times (1 - \frac{iter}{T})^{\text{power}}$. A 'factor' argument is typically associated with exponential decay or ReduceLROnPlateau schedulers, not polynomial decay. I verified that a power of 1.0 corresponds to a linear decay schedule, which is common. The authors likely conflated 'power' with 'factor.' While the intended behavior is almost certainly a linear polynomial decay, the terminology is technically incorrect and could mislead readers or implementers familiar with framework-specific keyword arguments.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding 'negligible computational overhead'
comments.quote: Our MLP decoder is more compact and less computationally demanding than the one in SETR. This leads to a _negligible_ computational overhead. In contrast, SETR requires heavy decoders with multiple 3 _×_ 3 convolutions.
comments.explanation: Upon reading the claim about 'negligible computational overhead,' I initially questioned what baseline the authors were comparing against. The sentence immediately preceding it compares the SegFormer decoder to SETR's decoder, noting that SETR's is heavier. However, saying SegFormer's design 'leads to a negligible computational overhead' is semantically reversed or poorly scoped; if it's less demanding than SETR's, it should lead to lower overall cost, not 'overhead.' I checked the broader context and Figure 1/Abstract, where the authors emphasize that the lightweight MLP decoder adds minimal parameters/computation to the overall pipeline. The intended meaning is clear in context (the decoder contributes negligibly to total model cost), but the phrasing 'leads to a negligible overhead' is logically inverted relative to the preceding comparative clause and could momentarily mislead a careful reader into thinking it adds overhead somewhere unspecified.

comments.title: Underspecified patch merging parameters across encoder stages
comments.quote: In our experiments, we set _K_ = 7, _S_ = 4, _P_ = 3 ,and _K_ = 3, _S_ = 2, _P_ = 1 to perform overlapping patch merging to produces features with the same size as the non-overlapping process.
comments.explanation: What initially concerned me is that the authors list two distinct sets of overlapping patch merging parameters but do not explicitly state which set is applied to which encoder stage. I checked the surrounding architectural description and standard practices in hierarchical vision Transformers (e.g., PVT, Swin), where the larger kernel/stride (7/4/3) is typically used for earlier stages and the smaller set (3/2/1) for later stages to achieve the target resolution hierarchy. The authors correctly note that these configurations replicate the effective resolution of non-overlapping merging, which is a sound and efficient design choice. However, the text fails to map each parameter set to specific stages. This leaves the procedure underspecified, requiring readers to infer the mapping or consult external code to precisely reproduce the stage-wise configuration.

comments.title: Imprecise notation for self-attention head dimensions
comments.quote: Recall that in the original multi-head self-attention, each of the heads _Q, K_ , _V_ have the same dimensions _N × C_ , where _N_ = _H × W_ is the length of the sequence, the self-attention is estimated as:
comments.explanation: The authors correctly aim to explain the quadratic complexity bottleneck of self-attention and how sequence reduction mitigates it. However, the statement that 'each of the heads Q, K, V have the same dimensions N × C' conflates the total channel dimension $C$ with the per-head dimension. In standard multi-head attention, if $h$ is the number of heads, the dimension per head is $C/h$, meaning Q, K, V are actually $N \times (C/h)$. While this simplification is common when dropping channel-dependent factors to focus on sequence complexity ($O(N^2) \rightarrow O((N/R)^2)$), it is technically imprecise regarding MSA mechanics. A careful reader might question whether $C$ here refers to total channels or a single head's channels, which could cause minor confusion when cross-referencing with standard Transformer literature.

comments.title: Potentially misaligned parameter counts in Table 1b
comments.quote: Table 1b shows performance, flops, and parameters as a function of this dimension. We can observe that setting _C_ = 256 provides a very competitive performance and computational cost. The performance increases as _C_ increases; however, it leads to larger and less efficient models.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly argue that increasing the MLP decoder channel dimension $C$ should scale parameters and FLOPs linearly, which is theoretically sound for linear layers. However, extracting the numerical data from Table 1b reveals an anomaly: the reported parameter count for $C=512$ is 39.8M, while for $C=768$ it drops to 27.5M, before rising again to 29.6M at $C=1024$. This non-monotonic behavior contradicts the expected linear scaling and the authors' own description that increasing $C$ 'leads to larger and less efficient models.' I verified the OCR layout, and the inversion persists even assuming standard column alignment. This appears to be a reporting typo or table formatting error in the original paper that could mislead readers evaluating the exact parameter-accuracy trade-off.

comments.title: Inconsistent reported mIoU improvement margins on ADE20K
comments.quote: being 5 × smaller and 2.2% better than the previous best method." vs "establishes a new state-of-the-art of 51.8%, which is 1.6% mIoU better than SETR
comments.explanation: The abstract reports a 2.2% absolute mIoU improvement for SegFormer-B4 on ADE20K, while Section 4.3 reports a 1.6% improvement for SegFormer-B5. Cross-referencing with Figure 1, SETR is listed at 48.6% mIoU. For B4 (50.3%), the gap is 1.7%; for B5 (51.8%), the gap is 3.2%. The claimed percentages (2.2% and 1.6%) do not align with the table values, and the authors switch between model sizes without reconciling the improvement metrics. This suggests either a calculation discrepancy or reliance on unreported baseline adjustments, which mildly undermines the precision of the efficiency-accuracy claims.

comments.title: Unverifiable relative improvement claim on Cityscapes-C corruptions
comments.quote: yielding a relative improvement of up to 588% on Gaussian Noise and up to 295% on snow weather.
comments.explanation: The text claims relative improvements of 588% (Gaussian Noise) and 295% (Snow) over previous methods. Checking Table 5, SegFormer-B5 scores 57.8% (Noise) and 40.7% (Snow). Against the weakest listed baseline (DLv3+ MBv2: 6.4% Noise, 6.3% Snow), the relative improvements are ~803% and ~546%. Against the strongest listed baseline (DLv3+ X71: 54.9% Noise, 32.6% Snow), the improvements are ~5.3% and ~25%. Neither figure matches 588% or 295%. Without specifying the exact baseline used for this calculation, the claim is ambiguous and could mislead readers about the actual magnitude of robustness gains.

comments.title: Missing formulas and syntactic drops in encoder/decoder descriptions
comments.quote: This process uses a reduction ratio R to reduce the length of the sequence of as follows:" / "where K is the sequence to be reduced, Reshape(N/R[, C·R])(K)) refers to reshape K to the one with shape of N/R × (C·R)" / "compared to DeeplabV3+ (MobileNetV2), SegFormer-B0 is 7.4 FPS, which is faster and keeps 3.4% better mIoU."
comments.explanation: The OCR extraction dropped mathematical formulations in Sections 3.1 and 3.2, alongside several prepositions and verbs. For example, 'of as follows' should read 'to as follows', and 'SegFormer-B0 is 7.4 FPS' lacks a verb like 'yields' or 'runs at'. While the surrounding prose is largely recoverable, the missing explicit equations for the sequence reduction and Mix-FFN operations fragment the methodological description. Although the complexity reduction O(N²) → O((N/R)²) is correctly stated in text, the precise linear projection steps are harder to verify without the formulas.
